message.content:
Adversarially review implemented task T140 ROUND 2 (goal G11, defect D40). Return the structured verdict JSON {taskId, verdict:"approve"|"disapprove", criticism:[], questions:[], defects:[], rationale, summary} and WRITE NOTHING to the ledger.

Fetch acceptance: `mcp__ledger__fetch_item` ledger=tasks item=T140.
Branch implement/T140-r2 at commit c1139e4e02f732d51fc2a570560fadd5ad4474c6, based at main a0e8b3d. Worktree: /home/pavel/work/safe/fecalia/.claude/worktrees/agent-a7b381371baa3e31f.
Diff: `git -C /home/pavel/work/safe/fecalia diff a0e8b3d c1139e4e02f732d51fc2a570560fadd5ad4474c6`.

CONTEXT: YOU raised the round-1 criticism: pathsock.go:31-33's relocated parenthetical implied the daemon holds CAP_NET_RAW pre-5.7, contradicting the CAP_NET_ADMIN-only units. Verify your criticism is RESOLVED: (1) the reworded pathsock.go comment no longer implies the daemon holds CAP_NET_RAW pre-5.7 (it states the units grant only CAP_NET_ADMIN so a pre-5.7 daemon also takes the source-IP fallback; unit tests bind loopback unprivileged); (2) `grep -rn CAP_NET_RAW internal/ packaging/ docs/` — ALL surfaces agree, none implies daemon CAP_NET_RAW; (3) comment/docs-only, CapabilityBoundingSet unchanged; (4) nothing new wrong. Run `cd /home/pavel/work/safe/fecalia/.claude/worktrees/agent-a7b381371baa3e31f && nix develop -c sh -c 'go build ./... && go vet ./... && go test ./...' && nix develop -c just lint`. If resolved and nothing new wrong, verdict approve. Split criticism[] vs questions[]; file genuine defects.

attachment.content:
- baboon: Baboon data model compiler reference. Covers model syntax, multi-version evolution, code generation, wire-format conversions, database migrations, backward compatibility, and version negotiation. TRIGGER when: designing, changing, or discussing any external API, working with .baboon model files, updating data models, adding new fields or types, bumping model versions, running code generation, implementing conversions, writing database migrations, or discussing API versioning strategy.
- constructive-test-taxonomy: Multi-axis classification of tests (Intention × Encapsulation × Isolation) for evaluating test quality and steering test-writing decisions. Replaces fuzzy Unit/Integration/E2E vocabulary with precise tags and a maintenance-cost heuristic. TRIGGER when: writing new tests, reviewing a test suite, deciding what to test or at what level, discussing mocks vs. real dependencies, classifying tests, or arguing about test coverage or test-strategy tradeoffs.
- dual-tests: Pattern for testing code that touches external systems: write one abstract test suite against an interface, then run it against both the production adapter (real DB/API/FS) and a hand-written in-memory dummy. Prefers manual dummies over auto-generated mocks. TRIGGER when: designing code that depends on a database, HTTP API, message queue, filesystem, or other external system; choosing between mocks, fakes, or dummies; adding integration points; setting up test infrastructure; or when the `constructive-test-taxonomy` skill calls for moving Communication-tier tests toward Blackbox-Atomic.
- environment: Bubblewrap "yolo" sandbox escape workflow (exchange scripts for reaching $HOME, system paths, or files outside the sandbox) plus in-sandbox nix/direnv usage. TRIGGER only when $SMIND_SANDBOXED=1 — i.e. running inside the yolo sandbox and you need to reach something outside it. SKIP entirely when $SMIND_SANDBOXED is unset: you are unsandboxed, so write directly and ignore this skill.
- izumi: Best-practice playbook for the izumi Scala ecosystem — distage (DI), distage-framework (roles/RoleAppMain), distage-testkit (test harness with memoization), distage-framework-docker (test containers), logstage (structured logging), and BIO (bifunctor effect typeclasses). Covers idiomatic bindings, Activation/Axis switching, Lifecycle discipline, plugin layout, and how to apply the `dual-tests` pattern inside distage so one test body runs against both a dummy and a real-DB adapter. TRIGGER when: writing or reviewing Scala code that imports `izumi.distage.*`, `izumi.logstage.*`, `izumi.functional.bio.*`, `distage.*`, `logstage.*`; designing a new distage plugin or Role; wiring a testkit `SpecZIO`/`Spec2`/`Spec1`; configuring `ContainerDef`/`DockerSupportModule`; choosing between `StandardAxis.Repo` or `Scene` tags; or implementing the dual-tests pattern in a distage project. The canonical reference project is https://github.com/7mind/distage-example.
- resilient-ws-ui: Guidelines for building applications that maintain reliable WebSocket connections to a backend and surface connection health clearly to the user. Invoke ONLY when the user explicitly references this skill by name (e.g. "/resilient-ws-ui", "use the resilient-ws-ui skill"). Do NOT auto-invoke based on topic match.
- tass: Target/Actual State Separation (TASS) pattern for reliable state management in event-driven control systems. TRIGGER when: implementing device or entity state management, designing control systems, working with target vs actual state separation, or building event-driven systems with commanded/observed state.
- cq:advance: Advance the WHOLE flow one cycle — chain /cq:investigate:advance, /cq:plan:advance, /cq:implement:advance to quiescence, then report DRAINED / BLOCKED-ON-QUESTIONS / MIXED.
- cq:implement-review: Implement-flow adversarial per-task review rubric + stdout-JSON contract, as a portable promptTemplate for non-Claude harnesses (pi/Codex). Symmetric to cq:plan-review. Judges ONE task's implementation, splits findings into autonomously-fixable criticism[], user-only questions[], and out-of-scope defects[], and emits the verdict as JSON on stdout. Writes NOTHING to the ledger — the orchestrator records the terminal review.
- cq:implement:advance: Advance an implement-flow run one full pass — pick DAG-ready tasks, implement each in an isolated worktree, adversarially review, autonomously fix criticism, register user questions, and merge back in dependency order.
- cq:implement:start: Start an implement-flow run — resolve the target milestones (default: all open ones), validate their task DAG, then hand off to the /cq:implement:advance loop.
- cq:investigate: Start an investigate-flow run — defect intake + bootstrap, then run the /cq:investigate:advance pass inline.
- cq:investigate:advance: Advance an investigate-flow run one research round — read defect state, form/extend the hypothesis tree, dispatch read-only explorers, validate every citation against source, adjudicate node status, and on a confirmed root cause file-and-defer the fix to plan-flow.
- cq:plan: Start a plan-flow goal — create the goal (from free text OR one-or-more idea-ids, one goal per idea), then hand off to the planner for the first clarifying questions.
- cq:plan-review: Shared adversarial plan-review rubric — judge an emitted plan for a goal against the fine-grained / sequenced / testable / grounded / complete rubric, classify findings into new_questions / criticism / defects, and emit a single fenced-json verdict on stdout. The CANONICAL rubric source; Claude's plan-reviewer agent and every non-Claude (Codex/Pi) reviewer judge the same way and emit the same JSON.
- cq:plan:advance: Advance plan-flow goals one full round — a given goal, or (no argument) every unlocked goal — running the planner↔reviewer loop until each needs the user or reaches `planned`.
- cq:plan:follow-up: Add new scope to an EXISTING plan-flow goal — append the follow-up request, re-open the goal, and hand to the planner for a fresh clarifying round.
- cq:planners: Session-only planner-set override — parse a natural-language planner instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_planners/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- cq:reviewers: Session-only reviewer-set override — parse a natural-language reviewer instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_reviewers/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- codex:rescue: Delegate investigation, an explicit fix request, or follow-up rescue work to the Codex rescue subagent
- codex:setup: Check whether the local Codex CLI is ready and optionally toggle the stop-time review gate
- codex:codex-cli-runtime: Internal helper contract for calling the codex-companion runtime from Claude Code
- codex:codex-result-handling: Internal guidance for presenting Codex helper output back to the user
- codex:gpt-5-4-prompting: Internal guidance for composing Codex and GPT-5.4 prompts for coding, review, diagnosis, and research tasks inside the Codex Claude Code plugin
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

input.description: Show full diff of T140 round-2 branch

content.content:
diff --git a/docs/install.md b/docs/install.md
index 6ee56af..74f2e08 100644
--- a/docs/install.md
+++ b/docs/install.md
@@ -871,6 +871,16 @@ systemctl enable --now wanbond-edge      # or wanbond-concentrator
 - The units run with `CapabilityBoundingSet=CAP_NET_ADMIN` (TUN creation) plus
   standard hardening. If you set `wireguard.listen_port` below 1024, add
   `CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE` to the bounding set.
+- Device-bind paths (`bind = "auto"` or `"device"`, `SO_BINDTODEVICE`) need no
+  further capability on Linux >=5.7: since kernel commit c427bfec18f21 ("net:
+  core: enable SO_BINDTODEVICE for non-root users"), binding a not-yet-bound
+  socket to a device requires no capability at all — empirically confirmed
+  (D40) with `CapabilityBoundingSet=CAP_NET_ADMIN` unchanged. Pre-5.7 kernels
+  need `CAP_NET_RAW`, which these units deliberately don't grant; on such a
+  kernel device-bind fails with EPERM and the daemon transparently falls back
+  to source-IP binding (see [install.md §3b](#3b-policy-routing-edge-topologies-source-ip-pinning-with-bind--source)
+  for when that fallback matters). All kernels this project supports
+  (Debian bookworm 6.1+, Ubuntu 22.04 5.15+) are already >=5.7.
 
 ### NetworkManager unmanaged-devices drop-in
 
diff --git a/internal/bind/pathsock.go b/internal/bind/pathsock.go
index bbdc134..4c49827 100644
--- a/internal/bind/pathsock.go
+++ b/internal/bind/pathsock.go
@@ -28,10 +28,14 @@ import (
 // no other path contends for the device (see selectDeviceBinds). When dev is ""
 // the socket pins the specific source IP — the pre-T16 behaviour.
 //
-// Device binding is also BEST-EFFORT even when selected: SO_BINDTODEVICE needs
-// CAP_NET_RAW (the daemon runs privileged, but the unit tests bind loopback
-// unprivileged) and is Linux-only, so a device-bind failure falls back to
-// source-IP binding rather than failing Open.
+// Device binding is also BEST-EFFORT even when selected: on Linux <5.7,
+// SO_BINDTODEVICE needs CAP_NET_RAW, which the shipped systemd units don't
+// grant (CapabilityBoundingSet is CAP_NET_ADMIN only), so a pre-5.7 daemon
+// also falls back to source-IP binding; on >=5.7 it needs no capability at
+// all (see bindToDevice, pathsock_linux.go, D40). The unit tests bind
+// loopback unprivileged too, exercising that same fallback path. It is also
+// Linux-only, so a device-bind failure falls back to source-IP binding
+// rather than failing Open.
 func listenPath(src netip.Addr, port uint16, dev string) (*net.UDPConn, error) {
 	if dev != "" {
 		if c, err := listenOnDevice(src, port, dev); err == nil {
diff --git a/internal/bind/pathsock_linux.go b/internal/bind/pathsock_linux.go
index 76c31b9..caba8e5 100644
--- a/internal/bind/pathsock_linux.go
+++ b/internal/bind/pathsock_linux.go
@@ -6,8 +6,15 @@ import "syscall"
 
 // bindToDevice pins the socket to the named interface via SO_BINDTODEVICE, so its
 // egress and ingress are confined to that device regardless of the source address
-// currently assigned to it. It requires CAP_NET_RAW; a permission error is
-// returned to the caller, which falls back to source-IP binding.
+// currently assigned to it. On Linux >=5.7 (kernel commit c427bfec18f21, "net:
+// core: enable SO_BINDTODEVICE for non-root users") this needs no capability at
+// all for a not-yet-bound socket, which is the only case this daemon exercises;
+// empirically confirmed (D40) with SO_BINDTODEVICE succeeding under a
+// zero-capability, non-root process on a 6.17 kernel. Pre-5.7 kernels still
+// require CAP_NET_RAW, which the shipped systemd units don't grant (only
+// CAP_NET_ADMIN). Either way, a permission error (EPERM, on a pre-5.7 kernel
+// lacking that capability) is returned to the caller, which falls back to
+// source-IP binding.
 func bindToDevice(fd uintptr, dev string) error {
 	return syscall.SetsockoptString(int(fd), syscall.SOL_SOCKET, syscall.SO_BINDTODEVICE, dev)
 }
diff --git a/packaging/systemd/wanbond-concentrator.service b/packaging/systemd/wanbond-concentrator.service
index a8b424b..b37ad98 100644
--- a/packaging/systemd/wanbond-concentrator.service
+++ b/packaging/systemd/wanbond-concentrator.service
@@ -27,6 +27,11 @@ RestartSec=2s
 # The daemon needs only CAP_NET_ADMIN (TUN device creation); it does no
 # interface addressing or routing itself (see docs/install.md). Add
 # CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE here only if wireguard.listen_port is below 1024.
+# Device-bind sockets (SO_BINDTODEVICE, bind = "auto"/"device") need no extra
+# capability here: on Linux >=5.7 SO_BINDTODEVICE requires none at all for a
+# not-yet-bound socket; pre-5.7 needs CAP_NET_RAW, which this unit doesn't
+# grant, so a pre-5.7 daemon falls back to source-IP binding on EPERM (see
+# internal/bind/pathsock_linux.go, D40).
 CapabilityBoundingSet=CAP_NET_ADMIN
 DeviceAllow=/dev/net/tun rw
 NoNewPrivileges=yes
diff --git a/packaging/systemd/wanbond-edge.service b/packaging/systemd/wanbond-edge.service
index e4d85b8..ac44d8c 100644
--- a/packaging/systemd/wanbond-edge.service
+++ b/packaging/systemd/wanbond-edge.service
@@ -20,7 +20,12 @@ Restart=on-failure
 RestartSec=2s
 
 # The daemon needs only CAP_NET_ADMIN (TUN device creation); it does no
-# interface addressing or routing itself (see docs/install.md).
+# interface addressing or routing itself (see docs/install.md). Device-bind
+# sockets (SO_BINDTODEVICE, bind = "auto"/"device") need no extra capability
+# here: on Linux >=5.7 SO_BINDTODEVICE requires none at all for a not-yet-bound
+# socket; pre-5.7 needs CAP_NET_RAW, which this unit doesn't grant, so a
+# pre-5.7 daemon falls back to source-IP binding on EPERM (see
+# internal/bind/pathsock_linux.go, D40).
 CapabilityBoundingSet=CAP_NET_ADMIN
 DeviceAllow=/dev/net/tun rw
 NoNewPrivileges=yes

content.content:
Web search results for query: "kernel commit c427bfec18f2 "net: core: enable SO_BINDTODEVICE for non-root users""

Links: [{"title":"net: core: enable SO_BINDTODEVICE for non-root users - linux-rng - Development tree for the kernel CSPRNG","url":"https://git.zx2c4.com/linux-rng/commit/net?h=jd/vdso-test-harness&id=c427bfec18f2190b8f4718785ee8ed2db4f84ee6"},{"title":"Diff - 43a5ead9254d686c58a74762bc6e25438b2f8dd0^! - kernel/common - Git at Google","url":"https://android.googlesource.com/kernel/common/+/43a5ead9254d686c58a74762bc6e25438b2f8dd0%5E!"},{"title":"[PATCH 5/5] udp: Handle errors on UDP sockets - David Gibson","url":"https://archives.passt.top/passt-dev/20240716052936.1204164-6-david@gibson.dropbear.id.au/"},{"title":"[PATCH v2 2/2] conf, icmp, tcp, udp: Add options to bind to outbound address and interface - dev - passt","url":"https://lists.seitan.rocks/hyperkitty/list/passt-dev@passt.top/message/TOXZGOA377LWUPXP5G4MJIKVAMPM4XC3/"},{"title":"[net-next,v2] net: core: enable SO_BINDTODEVICE for non-root users - Patchwork","url":"https://patchwork.ozlabs.org/patch/1264748/"},{"title":"[PATCH net-next v2] net: core: enable SO_BINDTODEVICE for non-root users — Netdev","url":"https://www.spinics.net/lists/netdev/msg641966.html"},{"title":"PATCH: IO: Avoid calling SO_BINDTODEVICE if not needed","url":"https://www.mail-archive.com/bird-users@network.cz/msg08201.html"}]

Based on the search results, here's information about kernel commit c427bfec18f2:

## Overview

This commit changes SO_BINDTODEVICE from requiring CAP_NET_RAW to allowing non-root users to bind a socket to an interface if it is not already bound.

## Purpose and Use Cases

This is useful to allow an application to bind itself to a specific VRF for outgoing or incoming connections. Previously, IP_UNICAST_IF and IPV6_UNICAST_IF were added for Wine specifically for use by non-root processes, but they are restricted to sendmsg() and not usable with TCP. Allowing SO_BINDTODEVICE allows TCP clients to get the same privilege.

## Key Limitations

The change has important constraints. When an application is restricted to a VRF (with `ip vrf exec`), the socket is bound to an interface at creation and therefore, a non-privileged call to SO_BINDTODEVICE to escape the VRF fails. Additionally, when an application bound a socket to SO_BINDTODEVICE and transmits it to a non-privileged process through a Unix socket, an attempt to change the bound device also fails.

This functionality has been available since kernel version 5.7.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Based on the search results, here's information about kernel commit c427bfec18f2:

## Overview

This commit changes SO_BINDTODEVICE from requiring CAP_NET_RAW to allowing non-root users to bind a socket to an interface if it is not already bound.

## Purpose and Use Cases

This is useful to allow an application to bind itself to a specific VRF for outgoing or incoming connections. Previously, IP_UNICAST_IF and IPV6_UNICAST_IF were added for Wine specifically for use by non-root processes, but they are restricted to sendmsg() and not usable with TCP. Allowing SO_BINDTODEVICE allows TCP clients to get the same privilege.

## Key Limitations

The change has important constraints. When an application is restricted to a VRF (with `ip vrf exec`), the socket is bound to an interface at creation and therefore, a non-privileged call to SO_BINDTODEVICE to escape the VRF fails. Additionally, when an application bound a socket to SO_BINDTODEVICE and transmits it to a non-privileged process through a Unix socket, an attempt to change the bound device also fails.

This functionality has been available since kernel version 5.7.

input.command: cd /home/pavel/work/safe/fecalia/.claude/worktrees/agent-a7b381371baa3e31f && git log -1 --format='%B' c1139e4 && grep -n '3b-policy-routing\|### 3b' docs/install.md | head -5
input.description: Show commit message and verify install.md section 3b anchor exists

content.content:
docs(D40): reconcile SO_BINDTODEVICE capability story across pathsock/units/install.md

Empirically confirmed on o3.7mind.io (kernel 6.17.0-1018-oracle, aarch64):
SO_BINDTODEVICE succeeds for a not-yet-bound socket under a zero-capability,
non-root process (uid 65534 "nobody", Current capability set empty). This
matches upstream kernel commit c427bfec18f21 ("net: core: enable
SO_BINDTODEVICE for non-root users"), landed in Linux 5.7 (June 2020): binding
an unbound socket to a device needs no capability at all on >=5.7; only
pre-5.7 kernels require CAP_NET_RAW. All kernels this project supports
(Debian bookworm 6.1+, Ubuntu 22.04 5.15+) are already >=5.7.

Update the bindToDevice comment (pathsock_linux.go), the listenPath BEST-EFFORT
comment (pathsock.go), both systemd unit files' capability comments, and
docs/install.md's systemd-units section to state the kernel-version-qualified
rule (>=5.7 needs nothing, <5.7 needs CAP_NET_RAW) with the EPERM -> source-IP
fallback documented. CapabilityBoundingSet is left unchanged in both units
(still CAP_NET_ADMIN only) since the probe shows CAP_NET_RAW is not actually
needed on any supported kernel.

Round-2 fix (D40 review criticism): the round-1 pathsock.go comment worded the
<5.7 clause as "(the daemon runs privileged, but the unit tests bind loopback
unprivileged)", which read as implying the daemon holds CAP_NET_RAW pre-5.7.
It doesn't — the shipped units grant only CAP_NET_ADMIN. Reworded every
surface (pathsock.go, pathsock_linux.go, both unit files, install.md) to
state explicitly that the units don't grant CAP_NET_RAW, so a pre-5.7 daemon
also takes the EPERM -> source-IP fallback; verified with
`grep -rn CAP_NET_RAW internal/ packaging/ docs/` that no surface implies
otherwise.

Probe: ssh -i /run/agenix/llm-ssh-key ubuntu@o3.7mind.io, gcc-compiled C probe
opening a UDP socket and calling setsockopt(SO_BINDTODEVICE, "lo") as uid 65534
(sudo -u nobody), with `capsh --print` confirming Current: = (no capabilities
held). Output: "SO_BINDTODEVICE(lo) SUCCEEDED". Also reproduced running
unelevated as the ambient ubuntu user (no ambient capabilities either).

Co-Authored-By: Claude Fable 5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

499:### 3b. Policy-routing edge topologies: source-IP pinning with `bind = "source"`
881:  to source-IP binding (see [install.md §3b](#3b-policy-routing-edge-topologies-source-ip-pinning-with-bind--source)

499:### 3b. Policy-routing edge topologies: source-IP pinning with `bind = "source"`
881:  to source-IP binding (see [install.md §3b](#3b-policy-routing-edge-topologies-source-ip-pinning-with-bind--source)

content.text:
All verification points pass. Evidence summary:

1. **Round-1 criticism resolved** — pathsock.go:31-36 now reads "SO_BINDTODEVICE needs CAP_NET_RAW, which the shipped systemd units don't grant (CapabilityBoundingSet is CAP_NET_ADMIN only), so a pre-5.7 daemon also falls back to source-IP binding". No implication the daemon holds CAP_NET_RAW; unit-test loopback note retained as exercising the same fallback path.
2. **All surfaces agree** — `grep -rn CAP_NET_RAW internal/ packaging/ docs/` returns exactly 5 hits (pathsock.go, pathsock_linux.go, both unit files, install.md), each stating the same rule: >=5.7 needs no capability for a not-yet-bound socket, pre-5.7 needs CAP_NET_RAW which the units don't grant, EPERM falls back to source-IP binding.
3. **Comment/docs-only** — the diff touches only comment lines in the two .go files, comment lines in the two .service files, and install.md prose; both `CapabilityBoundingSet=CAP_NET_ADMIN` lines are unchanged.
4. **Checks green** — `go build ./... && go vet ./... && go test ./...` all pass; `just lint` (vet + golangci-lint across all tag sets) reports 0 issues.
5. **Citations verified externally** — the cited kernel commit prefix `c427bfec18f21` matches upstream `c427bfec18f2190b8f4718785ee8ed2db4f84ee6` ("net: core: enable SO_BINDTODEVICE for non-root users", landed v5.7), and the comments correctly carry the "not-yet-bound socket" qualifier that the upstream change is scoped to. The install.md cross-link anchor `#3b-policy-routing-edge-topologies-source-ip-pinning-with-bind--source` resolves to the real §3b heading (docs/install.md:499).
6. **Probe documented** — the commit message records the acceptance-required probe: o3.7mind.io, kernel 6.17.0-1018-oracle aarch64, C probe under uid 65534 with `capsh --print` confirming an empty capability set, output "SO_BINDTODEVICE(lo) SUCCEEDED".

Sources: [upstream commit c427bfec18f2](https://git.zx2c4.com/linux-rng/commit/net?h=jd/vdso-test-harness&id=c427bfec18f2190b8f4718785ee8ed2db4f84ee6), [netdev patch v2](https://patchwork.ozlabs.org/patch/1264748/)

### Session summary
- **Did:** reviewed task T140 round 2 (diff a0e8b3d..c1139e4)
- **Achieved:** verdict approve; 0 criticisms / 0 questions
- **Discovered:** round-1 wording criticism fixed on every surface; kernel-commit citation and semantics verified against upstream; build/vet/test/lint all green; CapabilityBoundingSet untouched
- **Issues:** none

```json
{
  "taskId": "T140",
  "verdict": "approve",
  "criticism": [],
  "questions": [],
  "defects": [],
  "rationale": "Round-1 criticism resolved: pathsock.go now states the units grant only CAP_NET_ADMIN so a pre-5.7 daemon also takes the source-IP fallback. All 5 CAP_NET_RAW mentions across internal/, packaging/, docs/ state the same >=5.7 rule; diff is comment/docs-only with CapabilityBoundingSet unchanged; go build/vet/test and just lint green; kernel commit c427bfec18f21 verified against upstream v5.7.",
  "summary": "Approve round 2: reworded capability comments consistent on all surfaces, checks green, citation verified, no code changes."
}
```
